title: Trichilia aquifolia
intro: Trichilia aquifolia, also known as the Yellow Micol or Quinhão, is a plant species belonging to the Meliaceae family, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antioxidant effects, making it a valuable resource in traditional medicine. The bioactive constituents of Trichilia aquifolia include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various health conditions, including fever, cough, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Trichilia aquifolia is classified as a species of plant in the family Meliaceae. This family is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the mahogany family. The genus Trichilia is a group of trees and shrubs native to tropical and subtropical regions. Within the genus, Trichilia aquifolia is a specific species that is native to Central and South America. It is also known as the caimito or yellow caimito in some regions.
uses: Trichilia aquifolia, also known as the Guanacaste or Soapbark tree, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its bark is a source of saponins, which have been used to treat skin conditions and wounds. In some cultures, the plant's leaves and bark are used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other ailments. The plant's extracts have also been studied for their potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Additionally, Trichilia aquifolia is used in traditional medicine to treat digestive issues.
